items.text: We call our music 'electric church music,' because it's like a religion to us.
items.context: Hendrix explaining the spiritual dimension and religious significance of his music to a Canadian broadcaster.
items.publicReaction: This statement was embraced by the counterculture and music critics as evidence of music's spiritual role in the 1960s. It became a defining statement about Hendrix's artistic philosophy.

items.text: All I did was play it. I'm American, so I played it. I used to sing it in school, they made me sing it in school so — it was a flashback, you know.
items.context: Hendrix's explanation for his controversial rendition of the National Anthem at Woodstock, addressing the criticism and controversy it generated.
items.publicReaction: This explanation was widely reported and discussed in the press. It reframed his performance as patriotic rather than anti-American, though the controversy persisted in some circles.
items.source: The Dick Cavett Show (American television interview)

items.text: I listen to some of the things I've written and think 'damn.' Most of my writing was a clash between fantasy and reality and I felt you had to use fantasy to illuminate some aspects of reality.
items.context: Hendrix's artistic philosophy about the relationship between fantasy and reality in his songwriting and creative process, expressed during his final week of life.
items.publicReaction: This sophisticated articulation of his artistic method was recognized by critics as demonstrating Hendrix's intellectual approach to songwriting and his use of surrealism to convey deeper truths.
items.source: Final interview with Keith Altham (compiled in 'Hendrix on Hendrix: Interviews and Encounters' edited by Steven Roby, 2012)

items.text: Canada has given me the best Christmas present I ever had.
items.context: Hendrix's statement immediately after being acquitted of drug possession charges in Toronto, expressing gratitude for the court's decision.
items.publicReaction: This optimistic statement was reported in music and news media. It demonstrated Hendrix's resilience and maintained public image despite legal troubles that could have derailed his career.
items.source: Post-trial statement to reporters following acquittal (December 1969)
